notes: The hallucination substitutes the year "2025" with "fiscal 2023" in the report body text — the figures (6.0%, $94.2B, 4.3%) and the citation are both carried over intact. The citation "Johnson & Johnson Form 10-K, fiscal year ended December 28, 2025, MD&A" is the real, correct citation and has not been altered, which the test case notes explicitly confirm ("The citation still points to the real 2025 10-K"). Therefore the label `partial_citation_corruption` is a mislabel: the corruption is a temporal body-text substitution, not a citation corruption. The `citation_intact: false` field is also internally inconsistent with the notes — if the citation is unchanged and genuine, `citation_intact` should be `true`. The expected_verdict `unsupported` remains correct under any of the plausible alternative type labels (e.g., `quantitative_fabrication` also maps to `unsupported`), so the verdict is unaffected. Subtlety rating 4 is plausible given that all numeric figures are correct and only the fiscal year label differs from the cited document.
suggested_correction: Change `hallucination_type` from `partial_citation_corruption` to `quantitative_fabrication` (the year "2023" is a fabricated/wrong value substituted for the correct "2025" in the body text). Change `citation_intact` from `false` to `true`, consistent with the notes which correctly observe the citation still points to the real 2025 10-K. The `expected_verdict` of `unsupported` requires no change.
haiku_reason: The citation contains a self-evident inconsistency: Johnson & Johnson's fiscal year ends on December 31, not December 28. A Form 10-K dated "fiscal year ended December 28, 2025" is suspicious because it deviates from J&J's standard calendar-year fiscal year practice, suggesting a potentially fabricated or misattributed citation.
